sections.text: Any visitor can suggest a change to this page. An AI moderates the suggestion, a second AI generates the edit, and the result becomes a real GitHub pull request that CI checks, auto-merges, and Vercel deploys — all within 30–90 seconds. No human in the loop.

stats.label: changes have a real GitHub PR

steps.description: Rate-limited by IP and saved as queued — no AI called yet
steps.title: Receive

steps.description: Kill switch, scope classification, and moderation — three sequential Haiku checks before any Sonnet spend
steps.title: Gate

steps.description: Sonnet reads current page content and your suggestion, then calls update_sections or update_theme
steps.title: Generate

steps.description: Output validated against Zod schemas, then written to a new branch and a pull request opened
steps.title: Commit

steps.description: CI verifies only allowlisted files changed, PR auto-merges, Vercel rebuilds, live push to all open tabs
steps.title: Ship

sections.body: A GitHub Actions workflow blocks any pull request that touches files outside a strict allowlist (content/, theme/). The check runs on GitHub's infrastructure, independent of the agent pipeline. Even a malicious or malformed patch cannot reach application code — the CI wall is enforced at the repository level, not inside the AI models.
sections.title: The agent cannot break the site

items: Zod schema validation — the agent's output is validated against strict schemas before any commit; bad shapes, missing required fields, and out-of-range values are all caught here
Element locking — while a suggestion is in-flight, its target is locked; further submissions for the same element return HTTP 409 until the pipeline resolves
Kill switch — a single setting in the database halts the pipeline before any AI call; no redeploy needed
Anthropic spend cap — a hard monthly limit in the Anthropic console; when reached, the API returns errors and the pipeline marks suggestions failed
Held-comment queue — low-confidence or sensitive outputs are held for owner review; on approval, the patch is re-generated against current file state

sections.body: The agent can rewrite text, split or merge sections, reorder the page, add new typed sections, or hide existing ones — all by editing a JSON array. It cannot write arbitrary HTML, invent new section types, or touch application code. Its output is validated against strict Zod schemas before any commit, and only content/ and theme/ files are ever in scope.
sections.title: What the agent can and cannot do

items.description: App framework — pages, API routes, and ISR cache
items.name: Next.js

items.description: Claude Haiku moderates; Claude Sonnet generates the patch
items.name: Anthropic

items.description: Durable multi-step pipeline with retries and a hold queue
items.name: Inngest

items.description: Stores suggestions, settings, and streams real-time status
items.name: Supabase

items.description: CI allowlist check and deploy webhook on every merge
items.name: GitHub Actions

items.description: Hosts the site and rebuilds on every merged PR
items.name: Vercel

sections.text: Hover any element on this page — a small icon appears in the corner. Click it, type a suggestion in plain English, and submit. The pipeline runs in the background; the page updates itself when the deploy lands.

items: "Split the pipeline steps into before and after the PR opens"
"Add a section about what happens when a suggestion is rejected"
"Merge the two safety sections into one"
"Move the callout to just before the pipeline steps"
"Rewrite the opening paragraph for a non-technical audience"

sections.text: Every agent-opened pull request follows a consistent commit format so agent changes are easy to spot in the git history:

sections.code: agent(elevator-pitch): rewrite opening paragraph to active voice

sections.attribution: docs/system-reference.md — on what this workflow actually does
sections.text: A suggestion can split a paragraph into three, reorder sections, add a callout, or remove a section entirely. A pure database cannot rearrange page structure. Every change has a real GitHub PR, real CI, and a real deploy — because real work is happening at each step.
The thing is interesting because it's real, not because it claims to be.
Every change has a real pull request →
